title: 3D Navier-Stokes equations: the dynamics of a blow-up
speaker: Alexey P Cheskidov
affiliation: University of Illinois, Chicago; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will present an elementary introduction to the regularity/uniqueness issues for the Navier-Stokes equations. Thanks to the convex integration technique, the h-principle is starting to take shape for the uniqueness problem. It might be possible that classical scaling invariant uniqueness results (Prodi-Serrin type conditions) are the only constraints for constructing non-unique solutions of the NSE. As for the regularity problem, the situation is more delicate since there are also dynamical constraints that can potentially prevent solutions from blowing up.

proof_stages.label: Navier-Stokes regularity landscape
proof_stages.summary: The Navier-Stokes equations are set on the torus and the regularity/uniqueness problem is framed as an obstruction-versus-construction problem, with the Prodi-Serrin classes as the basic known positive region.

proof_stages.label: Fourier weak solutions and Leray-Hopf energy control
proof_stages.summary: The equations are rewritten in integrated Fourier form to define weak solutions, obtain global Leray-Hopf solutions by Galerkin compactness, and record the energy inequality and no-instantaneous-blow-up obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Blow-up as collective cascade
proof_stages.summary: Because each fixed Fourier coefficient remains a regular function, any singularity must arise from collective transfer of energy to infinitely high frequencies rather than from a single mode.

proof_stages.label: Energy-enstrophy worst-case phase portrait
proof_stages.summary: Tracking the kinetic energy and the H^1-size gives differential inequalities whose equality model has small-critical-norm decay, finite-time blow-up trajectories, and sharp divergence-free saturating paths.

proof_stages.label: Dyadic scale-local cascade model
proof_stages.summary: Decomposing into dyadic shell energies produces a local cascade ODE model whose blow-up or regularity depends on the effective dimension, and whose blow-up dynamics can be embedded in modified projected Navier-Stokes systems by turning off interactions.

proof_stages.label: Invariant-set regularity strategy and delayed cascades
proof_stages.summary: A possible route to ruling out blow-up is to find a positively invariant subcritical bounded set containing the origin, while delayed-cascade modifications show how even safer-looking models can be made to blow up.

proof_stages.label: Intermittent flux-maximizing fields
proof_stages.summary: Spatially intermittent vector fields concentrated in small eddies are identified as the way to saturate Bernstein and nonlinear flux estimates, leading to critical-space discontinuity and forced-blow-up constructions.

proof_stages.label: Convex integration with temporal intermittency
proof_stages.summary: The Reynolds-stress error is reduced by high-frequency perturbations, and for Navier-Stokes the use of spatial and temporal intermittency controls the viscous and high-mode errors to obtain nonunique rough solutions near Prodi-Serrin thresholds.

examples.name: Infinite-wheel energy cascade
examples.note: The chain of frictionally coupled wheels, later described as a hidden creature turning a wheel on tiny time intervals, is used to reveal how an infinite cascade can dissipate energy without visible work and to motivate temporal intermittency.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker builds the infinite-wheel cascade step by step, moving the handle, tracing energy transfer, heat production, and the zero-work/infinite-scale effect

passes.reason: an infinite limiting cascade is used to show energy conservation can fail through transfer to infinitely small scales
An infinite limiting cascade is used to show anomalous dissipation/energy nonconservation can occur with zero visible work.
An infinite limiting chain of wheels is used to show that energy conservation can fail via cascade to infinitely small scales.
a limiting infinite cascade is used to show zero net work can still produce dissipation, so energy conservation can fail.
An infinite limiting cascade sends energy to arbitrarily small scales with no visible work, used to show conservation of energy can fail via anomalous dissipation.
An infinite limiting cascade is used as a pathological case where finite-dimensional energy/work conservation intuition fails.
An infinite limiting cascade is used to show the finite-dimensional work/energy-conservation intuition can fail by energy escaping through infinitely many modes.
An infinite limiting chain of wheels is used to show anomalous dissipation/energy conservation failure with no visible forcing.
It is a limiting infinite cascade used to show zero-work infinite systems can violate naive energy conservation.
a limiting infinite cascade with energy escaping through arbitrarily small scales is used to show that zero visible work need not imply energy conservation
An infinite limiting chain is used to show zero visible forcing need not conserve energy.
An infinite limiting cascade is used to show that naive finite-system energy conservation can fail through escape to infinitely many modes.

examples.name: Single sine wave as non-intermittent field
examples.note: A sine-like field occupying the whole space is contrasted with concentrated eddies to make concrete why non-intermittent fields fail to maximize the nonlinear flux.
examples.engagement_reason: the sine wave is cited as a non-intermittent field occupying the whole space with comparable norms, but no computation or construction is carried out on it

passes.reason: a pure sine mode is the fully non-intermittent endpoint occupying the whole space rather than a sporadic or degenerate object
A pure one-Fourier-mode sine field is a minimal, highly regular non-intermittent endpoint and is cited as a field that fails to maximize the flux.
A pure sine mode is a maximally regular, full-space non-intermittent extreme, used as the bad case that fails to maximize flux.
a pure sine mode is the maximally homogeneous/non-intermittent endpoint and is presented as a bad case for flux maximization.
A pure sine/Fourier mode is highly regular and maximally non-intermittent or space-filling, used as the bad case showing such fields do not maximize flux.
A pure single Fourier mode has collapsed spectral support and maximal non-intermittency, and is used as the foil showing such fields fail to maximize flux.
A pure sine wave is a maximally homogeneous non-intermittent Fourier mode, cited as a field that fails to produce the desired flux saturation.
A pure sine mode is a maximally non-intermittent, highly regular field used as the negative case that fails to produce optimal flux.
A pure sine/Fourier mode is maximally non-intermittent and is cited as a field that fails to give the desired flux-saturating construction.
a pure sine/Fourier mode is maximally homogeneous and sits at the non-intermittent endpoint, serving as the case that fails to maximize flux
A pure Fourier mode is a highly symmetric nonintermittent endpoint used as a bad case for flux saturation.
A single Fourier sine mode is the fully non-intermittent endpoint and is cited as a field that fails to produce the desired maximal flux.

examples.name: Burgers shock analogy
examples.note: In response to a question, Burgers shock formation is invoked as an analogy for how a nonlinear forward flux can spread energy into a regularizing power law.
examples.engagement_reason: Burgers shock is invoked only as an analogy for a power-law/regularizing cascade behavior, without working through the Burgers example

passes.reason: a Burgers shock is a discontinuous weak/singular solution used only as an analogy for cascade behavior
A Burgers shock is a singular collapsed weak-solution profile used only as an analogy for cascade/blow-up behavior.
The Burgers shock is a singular limiting weak-solution profile invoked only as an analogy, not to refute a claim.
the Burgers shock is invoked only as a standard analogy for shock/cascade behavior, not to refute a claim.
The Burgers shock is a standard conservation-law phenomenon invoked only as an analogy for cascade or shock behavior, not to refute a claim.
A Burgers shock is a singular weak-solution profile invoked only as an analogy for cascade behavior, not to refute a claim.
A Burgers shock is a singular limiting jump profile used only as an analogy for cascade/regularization, not to refute a claim.
The Burgers shock is invoked only as an analogy, and the shock itself is a singular limiting boundary case of smooth Burgers evolution.
The Burgers shock is a standard discontinuous weak singularity invoked only as an analogy for energy spreading.
an ordinary Burgers shock is invoked only as an analogy for forward transfer and power-law behavior
A standard nonsmooth Burgers shock is used only as an analogy for forward spectral spreading.
The Burgers shock is invoked only as a standard analogy for nonlinear forward cascade/regularization, not to refute a claim.
